questions.questionText: Simple lipids are best defined as:
options: Esters of fatty acids with various alcohols
Lipids containing phosphate and nitrogen base only
questions.explanation: Simple lipids are esters formed between fatty acids and alcohols. Examples include fats, oils, and waxes.

questions.questionText: Triacylglycerols are esters of fatty acids with:
questions.explanation: Triacylglycerols, also called triglycerides, are formed when three fatty acids are esterified to glycerol.

questions.questionText: A fat in the liquid state is called:
questions.explanation: Fats and oils are both triacylglycerols. The difference is mainly physical state: fats are solid, while oils are liquid.

questions.questionText: Waxes are esters of fatty acids with:
questions.explanation: Waxes are simple lipids made from fatty acids esterified with high molecular weight alcohols. Examples include cholesterol esters and vitamin A/D esters.

questions.questionText: Compound lipids differ from simple lipids because they contain:
options: Additional groups besides fatty acid and alcohol
questions.explanation: Compound lipids contain fatty acids and alcohol, but also additional groups such as phosphate, nitrogenous bases, carbohydrate, or protein components.

questions.questionText: Phospholipids contain fatty acids, alcohol, and:
questions.explanation: Phospholipids are compound lipids that contain phosphoric acid in addition to fatty acids and an alcohol.

questions.questionText: Glycolipids contain fatty acids with:
questions.explanation: Glycolipids contain fatty acids and carbohydrate. They also contain nitrogen but no phosphoric acid.

questions.questionText: Derived lipids include:
options: Fatty acids, glycerol, steroids, and ketone bodies
questions.explanation: Derived lipids are substances produced from simple or compound lipids by hydrolysis. Examples include fatty acids, glycerol, steroids, fatty aldehydes, ketone bodies, fat-soluble vitamins, and carotenoids.

questions.questionText: Neutral lipids are called neutral because they are:
questions.explanation: Acylglycerols, cholesterol, and cholesterol esters are called neutral lipids because they do not carry an electrical charge.

questions.questionText: More than 90% of dietary lipids are usually:
questions.explanation: Most dietary fat is triacylglycerol. Smaller fractions include cholesterol, cholesterol esters, phospholipids, and free fatty acids.

questions.questionText: In adults, lipid digestion in the mouth and stomach is generally:
options: The major site of lipid digestion
questions.explanation: Dietary lipids are not digested to a major extent in the mouth or stomach in adults. Most lipid digestion occurs in the small intestine.

questions.questionText: Gastric lipase is more important in:
questions.explanation: Gastric lipase can degrade milk lipids and has a role in infants, but it is not a major lipid-digesting enzyme in adults.

questions.questionText: The main site of dietary lipid digestion is the:
questions.explanation: Most lipid digestion occurs in the small intestine, where bile salts and pancreatic enzymes act together.

questions.questionText: Emulsification of dietary lipids is important because it:
options: Increases surface area for enzymatic digestion
questions.explanation: Lipids are water-insoluble, and enzymes can act mainly at the surface of lipid droplets. Emulsification breaks large lipid droplets into smaller droplets, increasing the surface area.

questions.questionText: Emulsification is mainly achieved by:
options: Bile salts and mechanical mixing by peristalsis
questions.explanation: Bile salts act like detergents, and peristalsis mechanically mixes intestinal contents. Together, they emulsify dietary fat.

questions.questionText: Bile salts help lipid digestion because they have:
questions.explanation: Bile salts are amphipathic molecules. Their detergent action helps emulsify lipid droplets and makes lipid digestion more efficient.

questions.questionText: Cholecystokinin is secreted mainly in response to:
questions.explanation: Cholecystokinin, or CCK, is released from the lower duodenum in response to lipids. It stimulates gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme secretion.

questions.questionText: CCK causes:
options: Gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme release
Complete closure of the intestinal lumen
questions.explanation: CCK promotes bile release from the gallbladder and digestive enzyme secretion from the pancreas, both important for lipid digestion.

questions.questionText: Secretin is released in response to:
options: High lipid storage in adipose tissue
questions.explanation: Secretin is released when acidic chyme enters the intestine. It stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion.

questions.questionText: Secretin stimulates secretion of:
questions.explanation: Bicarbonate neutralizes acidic chyme, creating a suitable pH for pancreatic digestive enzymes.

questions.questionText: Pancreatic lipase hydrolyzes triacylglycerol mainly at:
questions.explanation: Pancreatic lipase removes fatty acids from positions C1 and C3 of triacylglycerol, producing two free fatty acids and 2-monoacylglycerol.

questions.questionText: The main products of pancreatic lipase action on triacylglycerol are:
questions.explanation: Pancreatic lipase does not completely hydrolyze all ester bonds. It mainly produces free fatty acids from C1/C3 and leaves 2-monoacylglycerol.

questions.questionText: Colipase helps pancreatic lipase by:
questions.explanation: Colipase is secreted by the pancreas and helps pancreatic lipase remain active at the lipid-water interface.

questions.questionText: Cholesterol esterase hydrolyzes cholesterol esters to:
questions.explanation: Cholesterol esterase breaks cholesterol esters into free cholesterol and fatty acids.

questions.questionText: Phospholipase A2 removes a fatty acid from which position of phospholipids?
questions.explanation: Phospholipase A2 removes the fatty acid at carbon 2 of a phospholipid, producing a lysophospholipid.

questions.questionText: Phospholipase A2 is activated in the intestine by:
questions.explanation: The file states that phospholipase A2 is activated by trypsin in the intestine.

questions.questionText: Lecithin is another name for:
questions.explanation: Lecithin is phosphatidylcholine. When phospholipase A2 acts on it, lysolecithin is formed.

questions.questionText: The primary products of dietary lipid degradation absorbed by intestinal mucosal cells include:
options: Free fatty acids, free cholesterol, and 2-monoacylglycerol
questions.explanation: Dietary lipid digestion produces free fatty acids, free cholesterol, and 2-monoacylglycerol, which are absorbed by intestinal mucosal cells.

questions.questionText: Micelles are formed from lipid digestion products together with:
questions.explanation: Bile salts combine with lipid digestion products to form micelles. Micelles help transport lipids through the aqueous intestinal lumen to the brush border.

questions.questionText: Short- and medium-chain fatty acids differ from long-chain fatty acids because they:
options: Do not require micelle formation for absorption
Must be packaged into chylomicrons first
questions.explanation: Short- and medium-chain fatty acids are more water-soluble than long-chain fatty acids. They can be absorbed without needing micelle formation.

questions.questionText: Why are dietary lipids poorly digested in the mouth and stomach of adults?
options: Most lipid digestion requires bile salts and pancreatic enzymes in the small intestine
Lipids are completely water-soluble in the stomach
Gastric lipase is the main adult lipid-digesting enzyme
Pancreatic lipase is secreted into the mouth
questions.explanation: Adult lipid digestion occurs mainly in the small intestine because emulsification by bile salts and hydrolysis by pancreatic enzymes are needed for efficient digestion. Mouth and stomach digestion is limited.

questions.questionText: Why is gastric lipase more relevant in infants than adults?
options: It requires bile salts to work
questions.explanation: Gastric lipase can digest milk lipids and has a role in infants. In adults, dietary lipid digestion in the stomach is limited.

questions.questionText: Why is emulsification required before efficient enzymatic lipid digestion?
options: Lipid hydrolysis occurs mainly at the lipid-water interface
questions.explanation: Lipids are water-insoluble. Enzymes can access lipid droplets mainly at their surface. Emulsification increases surface area, allowing pancreatic enzymes to act more effectively.

questions.questionText: Which two mechanisms produce lipid emulsification in the small intestine?
options: Bile salt detergent action and intestinal peristalsis
questions.explanation: Bile salts act as detergents because they are amphipathic. Peristalsis mechanically mixes intestinal contents. Together, they break fat into smaller droplets.

questions.questionText: CCK is important in lipid digestion because it stimulates:
options: Gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme secretion
questions.explanation: CCK is released in response to lipids in the duodenum. It causes the gallbladder to contract and stimulates pancreatic digestive enzyme release.

questions.questionText: Secretin improves lipid digestion mainly by:
options: Stimulating pancreatic bicarbonate secretion to neutralize acidic chyme
Activating lipoprotein lipase in adipose tissue
Stimulating insulin release from beta cells only
questions.explanation: Secretin is released in response to low pH. Bicarbonate neutralizes acidic chyme and provides a suitable pH for pancreatic enzymes.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme specifically requires colipase for efficient activity at the lipid-water interface?
questions.explanation: Colipase anchors and stabilizes pancreatic lipase at the lipid-water interface, allowing lipase to hydrolyze triacylglycerols efficiently.

questions.questionText: Pancreatic lipase digestion of TAG mainly produces:
options: Free glycerol and three fatty acids only
questions.explanation: Pancreatic lipase hydrolyzes ester bonds at C1 and C3 of triacylglycerol, leaving 2-monoacylglycerol plus two free fatty acids.

questions.questionText: Cholesterol esterase is needed because dietary cholesterol often exists as:
questions.explanation: Cholesterol esterase hydrolyzes cholesterol esters into free cholesterol and fatty acid, allowing absorption.

questions.questionText: Phospholipase A2 converts phospholipids into:
questions.explanation: Phospholipase A2 removes the fatty acid at carbon 2 of phospholipids, producing a lysophospholipid and a free fatty acid.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme further removes the remaining fatty acid from lysophospholipid?
questions.explanation: After phospholipase A2 forms a lysophospholipid, lysophospholipase can remove the remaining fatty acid, producing a glycerylphosphoryl base.

questions.questionText: Which product pair is correctly matched with its digestive enzyme?
options: Cholesterol esterase → cholesterol + free fatty acid
questions.explanation: Cholesterol esterase hydrolyzes cholesterol esters. The products are free cholesterol and free fatty acid.

questions.questionText: Micelles are best described as:
options: Bile salt-containing particles that solubilize lipid digestion products
Mitochondrial carriers for fatty acid oxidation
questions.explanation: Micelles contain bile salts and lipid digestion products. Their hydrophobic interior carries lipids, while their hydrophilic exterior allows movement through intestinal fluid.

questions.questionText: Why are micelles amphipathic?
options: They have a hydrophobic inside and hydrophilic outside
They are made only of glucose
They are completely nonpolar on the outside
questions.explanation: Micelles organize lipid molecules so that hydrophobic parts are inside and hydrophilic parts face the aqueous intestinal lumen. This allows lipid transport to the brush border.

questions.questionText: Which digestion products are delivered by micelles to intestinal mucosal cells?
options: Free fatty acids, free cholesterol, and 2-monoacylglycerol
questions.explanation: Micelles transport lipid digestion products to the brush border membrane for absorption into intestinal mucosal cells.

questions.questionText: Short- and medium-chain fatty acids enter which route after absorption?
options: Portal circulation bound to serum albumin
Directly into bile salts for enterohepatic circulation
questions.explanation: Short- and medium-chain fatty acids do not require micelles or chylomicron packaging to the same extent. They enter portal circulation and are carried by albumin to the liver.

questions.questionText: Long-chain fatty acids absorbed by intestinal mucosal cells are first activated to:
questions.explanation: Inside intestinal mucosal cells, fatty acids are activated to acyl-CoA before being re-esterified into triacylglycerols and cholesterol esters.

questions.questionText: Re-esterification inside intestinal mucosal cells reforms:
questions.explanation: After absorption, lipid digestion products are rebuilt into hydrophobic storage/transport forms such as TAG and cholesterol esters.

questions.questionText: Because TAG and cholesterol esters are very hydrophobic, they must be packaged as:
questions.explanation: TAG and cholesterol esters are too hydrophobic to move freely in aqueous body fluids. Intestinal cells package them into chylomicrons for transport.

questions.questionText: Chylomicrons are assembled in:
questions.explanation: Chylomicrons are formed in intestinal mucosal cells after dietary lipid absorption and re-esterification.

questions.questionText: Nascent chylomicrons contain which apoprotein?
questions.explanation: Intestinal mucosal cells synthesize ApoB-48, which is required for chylomicron formation.

questions.questionText: If ApoB-48 is not synthesized, which disorder can occur?
questions.explanation: ApoB-48 is necessary for chylomicron formation. If it is absent, TAG accumulates in intestinal mucosal cells, causing abetalipoproteinemia.

questions.questionText: Chylomicrons leave intestinal mucosal cells first through:
questions.explanation: Chylomicrons are too large to enter portal capillaries directly. They are secreted into lymphatic vessels, pass through the thoracic duct, and then enter blood.

questions.questionText: Chylomicrons mainly transport:
questions.explanation: Chylomicrons carry dietary, or exogenous, triacylglycerol and other lipids from the intestine to tissues.

questions.questionText: Which lipoprotein mainly carries endogenous fat from liver to tissues?
questions.explanation: Chylomicrons carry exogenous dietary fat. VLDL carries endogenous triacylglycerol synthesized in the liver to peripheral tissues.

questions.questionText: A patient has impaired bile salt secretion. Which process is most directly impaired?
questions.explanation: Bile salts emulsify dietary lipids and form micelles. Without bile salts, digestion and absorption of long-chain lipids are impaired.

questions.questionText: Which lipid product would be least dependent on micelle formation?
questions.explanation: Short- and medium-chain fatty acids are relatively more water-soluble and can enter portal circulation without requiring micelle formation.

questions.questionText: A defect in pancreatic lipase would most directly reduce formation of:
options: 2-monoacylglycerol and free fatty acids from TAG
questions.explanation: Pancreatic lipase digests triacylglycerols at C1 and C3. A defect would impair formation of 2-monoacylglycerol and free fatty acids.

questions.questionText: A defect in colipase would mainly impair:
options: Stabilization of pancreatic lipase during TAG digestion
questions.explanation: Colipase supports pancreatic lipase function at the lipid-water interface. Without colipase, TAG digestion becomes less efficient.

questions.questionText: A defect in cholesterol esterase would most directly impair absorption of:
questions.explanation: Cholesterol esterase hydrolyzes cholesterol esters into free cholesterol and fatty acid. Free cholesterol is the absorbable form.

questions.questionText: A defect in phospholipase A2 would reduce formation of:
questions.explanation: Phospholipase A2 removes the fatty acid at carbon 2 of phospholipids, producing lysophospholipids.

questions.questionText: Which hormone best explains gallbladder contraction after a fatty meal?
questions.explanation: Lipids in the duodenum stimulate CCK release. CCK causes gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme release.

questions.questionText: Which hormone best explains bicarbonate-rich pancreatic secretion after acidic chyme enters the intestine?
questions.explanation: Secretin responds to low pH and stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion, neutralizing acid and optimizing enzyme activity.

questions.questionText: Which sequence correctly describes dietary TAG processing?
options: Emulsification → pancreatic lipase digestion → micelle formation → absorption → re-esterification → chylomicron formation
Chylomicron formation → stomach digestion → urea cycle → renal excretion
TCA cycle → micelles → bile synthesis → glucose absorption
Protein digestion → ApoB-100 formation → VLDL secretion
questions.explanation: Dietary lipid handling requires emulsification, enzymatic digestion, micellar transport, mucosal absorption, re-esterification, and packaging into chylomicrons.

questions.questionText: Which step occurs inside intestinal mucosal cells?
options: Re-synthesis of TAG and cholesterol esters
Hydrolysis by pancreatic lipase in the lumen
Activation of phospholipase A2 by trypsin
questions.explanation: After absorption, intestinal mucosal cells re-esterify fatty acids and monoacylglycerol to form TAG and cholesterol esters.

questions.questionText: Which apoprotein is required for chylomicron assembly in intestinal mucosal cells?
questions.explanation: ApoB-48 is synthesized by intestinal mucosal cells and is necessary for chylomicron formation.

questions.questionText: Which apoprotein is obtained by nascent chylomicrons from HDL and activates lipoprotein lipase?
questions.explanation: Nascent chylomicrons receive ApoC-II from HDL. ApoC-II activates lipoprotein lipase in capillaries.

questions.questionText: Which apoprotein helps chylomicron remnants bind hepatic receptors?
questions.explanation: Chylomicron remnants are recognized by liver receptors through ApoE together with ApoB-48.

questions.questionText: Lipoprotein lipase is located mainly:
options: On capillary walls of tissues such as adipose tissue and muscle
questions.explanation: Lipoprotein lipase is found on capillary walls, especially in adipose tissue and muscle, where it hydrolyzes TAG in chylomicrons and VLDL.

questions.questionText: Lipoprotein lipase hydrolyzes TAG in chylomicrons to produce:
options: Free fatty acids and glycerol/monoacylglycerol products
questions.explanation: LPL breaks down TAG carried by chylomicrons, releasing fatty acids for uptake by tissues.

questions.questionText: After most TAG is removed from chylomicrons, the remaining particles are called:
questions.explanation: As LPL removes TAG, chylomicrons become smaller and denser. The remaining particles are called chylomicron remnants.

questions.questionText: Chylomicron remnants are removed mainly by:
questions.explanation: Chylomicron remnants bind hepatic receptors and are taken up by the liver through receptor-mediated endocytosis.

questions.questionText: Chylomicron remnants bind liver receptors mainly through:
questions.explanation: Hepatocyte receptors recognize the combination of ApoB-48 and ApoE on chylomicron remnants.

questions.questionText: Which process occurs after chylomicron remnants enter hepatocytes?
options: Fusion with lysosomes and degradation to components
Conversion to bile salts in intestinal lumen only
questions.explanation: After endocytosis, chylomicron remnants fuse with lysosomes and are degraded into amino acids, free cholesterol, fatty acids, and glycerol.

questions.questionText: ApoC-II is returned to which particle after TAG removal from chylomicrons?
questions.explanation: During chylomicron metabolism, ApoC-II is transferred from HDL to chylomicrons, activates LPL, and then is returned to HDL.

questions.questionText: Which statement best distinguishes digestion from absorption in lipid handling?
options: Digestion hydrolyzes dietary lipids in the lumen; absorption moves lipid products into intestinal mucosal cells
Digestion occurs only after chylomicron secretion
Absorption occurs only in the stomach
Digestion and absorption are identical processes
questions.explanation: Digestion breaks lipids into absorbable products. Absorption transfers those products into intestinal mucosal cells for re-esterification and packaging.

questions.questionText: Which statement best distinguishes micelles from chylomicrons?
options: Micelles aid absorption in the intestinal lumen; chylomicrons transport re-esterified lipids from intestinal cells through lymph/blood
Micelles are secreted into lymph; chylomicrons remain in intestinal lumen
Chylomicrons are made only of bile salts
questions.explanation: Micelles are luminal transport structures formed with bile salts. Chylomicrons are lipoprotein particles assembled inside intestinal mucosal cells.

questions.questionText: Which statement best distinguishes long-chain fatty acid transport from short/medium-chain fatty acid transport?
options: Long-chain fatty acids are packaged into chylomicrons; short/medium-chain fatty acids go to portal blood bound to albumin
Short-chain fatty acids require ApoB-48 chylomicrons
Long-chain fatty acids go directly to portal blood only
All fatty acids require identical lymphatic transport
questions.explanation: Long-chain fatty acids are re-esterified and packaged into chylomicrons. Short- and medium-chain fatty acids can enter portal circulation more directly.

questions.questionText: A patient cannot synthesize ApoB-48. What is the expected intestinal problem?
options: TAG accumulates in intestinal mucosal cells due to impaired chylomicron formation
questions.explanation: ApoB-48 is required for chylomicron assembly. Without it, intestinal cells cannot efficiently export TAG, causing lipid accumulation.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why chylomicrons are secreted into lymph first?
options: They are large lipoprotein particles that enter lymphatics before reaching blood through the thoracic duct
They are small enough to enter portal blood directly
They are bile salts that must return to liver through enterohepatic circulation
They are absorbed only into RBCs
questions.explanation: Chylomicrons are large particles and leave intestinal mucosal cells through lymphatic vessels, then enter blood via the thoracic duct.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes HDL’s role in chylomicron maturation?
options: HDL donates ApoC-II and ApoE to nascent chylomicrons
HDL digests TAG directly inside the intestine
HDL forms micelles with bile salts
HDL removes C1 and C3 fatty acids from TAG
questions.explanation: Nascent chylomicrons receive ApoC-II and ApoE from circulating HDL. These apoproteins are essential for LPL activation and remnant recognition.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why ApoC-II deficiency resembles lipoprotein lipase deficiency?
options: ApoC-II is needed to activate LPL
ApoC-II is needed to form bile salts
ApoC-II is the structural protein of chylomicrons
questions.explanation: If ApoC-II is deficient, LPL cannot be properly activated. This impairs TAG hydrolysis in chylomicrons, similar to LPL deficiency.

questions.questionText: Deficiency of LPL or ApoC-II causes accumulation of:
questions.explanation: LPL hydrolyzes TAG in chylomicrons and VLDL. If LPL or its activator ApoC-II is deficient, TAG-rich particles accumulate.

questions.questionText: Familial hyperchylomicronemia is also known as:
questions.explanation: The file states that deficiency of lipoprotein lipase or ApoC-II causes accumulation of TAG-rich lipoproteins, leading to type I hyperlipidemia, also called familial hyperchylomicronemia.

questions.questionText: Heparin can be used experimentally to measure LPL because it:
questions.explanation: LPL is attached to capillary walls. Heparin can release it into plasma, allowing post-heparin LPL measurement.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains chylomicron remnant clearance?
options: Remnants are recognized by hepatic receptors through ApoE and ApoB-48 and taken up by endocytosis
Remnants are excreted unchanged in urine
Remnants are digested by pancreatic lipase in blood
Remnants return to the intestinal lumen
questions.explanation: After TAG removal, chylomicron remnants retain ApoE and ApoB-48. Hepatic receptors recognize these apoproteins and internalize the remnants.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why chylomicrons become smaller and denser during circulation?
options: LPL removes TAG from their core
They gain large amounts of TAG from tissues
They lose all protein coat immediately
Bile salts dissolve them in blood
questions.explanation: Chylomicrons are TAG-rich. As LPL hydrolyzes their TAG, the particles shrink and become relatively denser, forming remnants.

questions.questionText: Which statement best compares chylomicrons and VLDL?
options: Chylomicrons carry exogenous dietary fat; VLDL carries endogenous liver-made fat
Chylomicrons are made in liver; VLDL is made in intestine
VLDL transports dietary fat from intestine
questions.explanation: This is a very important distinction. Chylomicrons transport dietary lipids from the intestine, while VLDL transports liver-synthesized TAG to peripheral tissues.

questions.questionText: Which event occurs after most TAG is removed from chylomicrons?
options: ApoC-II returns to HDL and remnant is taken up by liver
questions.explanation: After LPL removes TAG, ApoC-II is returned to HDL. The remaining chylomicron remnant is cleared by the liver.

questions.questionText: Which integrated statement best summarizes dietary lipid transport?
options: Dietary lipids are digested in the intestine, absorbed as lipid products, re-esterified in mucosal cells, packaged with ApoB-48 into chylomicrons, secreted through lymph, and delivered to tissues by LPL
Dietary lipids are converted directly into glucose in the stomach
Dietary TAG enters portal blood unchanged and is used directly by RBCs
Chylomicrons are made in liver and carry endogenous fat only
questions.explanation: This captures the full pathway: digestion, micellar absorption, intracellular re-esterification, chylomicron assembly, lymphatic transport, blood delivery, LPL-mediated TAG hydrolysis, and hepatic remnant clearance.

questions.questionText: A patient has impaired bile salt secretion. Which dietary lipid process is most directly reduced?
options: Micelle formation and absorption of long-chain lipid digestion products
Direct absorption of medium-chain fatty acids into portal blood
ApoB-48 synthesis in intestinal mucosal cells
questions.explanation: Bile salts are required for emulsification and micelle formation. Without them, long-chain fatty acids, 2-monoacylglycerol, cholesterol, and other hydrophobic digestion products are poorly solubilized in the intestinal lumen, so absorption decreases.

questions.questionText: A patient has normal pancreatic enzymes but poor bile flow. Which step would still be relatively less affected?
options: Absorption of short- and medium-chain fatty acids
Micellar transport of long-chain fatty acids
questions.explanation: Short- and medium-chain fatty acids are more water-soluble and do not require micelle formation to the same extent. Long-chain lipid products depend much more on bile salt micelles for efficient absorption.

questions.questionText: A patient has defective pancreatic lipase. Which intestinal digestion products are most directly decreased?
options: 2-monoacylglycerol and free fatty acids from triacylglycerol
questions.explanation: Pancreatic lipase hydrolyzes TAG mainly at carbon 1 and carbon 3. The expected products are two free fatty acids and 2-monoacylglycerol. Cholesterol ester digestion and phospholipid digestion require different enzymes.

questions.questionText: A defect in colipase would cause fat maldigestion mainly because:
options: Pancreatic lipase cannot work efficiently at the lipid-water interface
questions.explanation: Colipase anchors and stabilizes pancreatic lipase on lipid droplets. Without colipase, pancreatic lipase activity is inefficient even if the enzyme itself is present.

questions.questionText: A defect in phospholipase A2 would most directly reduce formation of:
questions.explanation: Phospholipase A2 removes the fatty acid at carbon 2 of phospholipids. Therefore, impaired phospholipase A2 decreases lysophospholipid formation.

questions.questionText: A defect in cholesterol esterase would mainly impair:
options: Hydrolysis of cholesterol esters before absorption
Hydrolysis of TAG at C1 and C3
Formation of micelles from bile salts
questions.explanation: Cholesterol esterase converts cholesterol esters into free cholesterol and fatty acids. Free cholesterol is the form that is efficiently absorbed by intestinal mucosal cells.

questions.questionText: A patient cannot synthesize ApoB-48 in intestinal mucosal cells. What is the most likely consequence?
options: TAG accumulates in intestinal mucosal cells due to impaired chylomicron formation
Chylomicron remnants are cleared faster by liver
questions.explanation: ApoB-48 is essential for chylomicron assembly. Without ApoB-48, absorbed and re-esterified TAG cannot be packaged properly, leading to lipid accumulation in intestinal mucosal cells. This is the logic of abetalipoproteinemia.

questions.questionText: Which defect best explains abetalipoproteinemia in this topic?
options: Failure of ApoC-II to activate LPL
Failure of ApoE to bind liver receptors
Failure of albumin to carry medium-chain fatty acids
questions.explanation: The file links ApoB-48 absence with failure of chylomicron formation and accumulation of TAG in intestinal mucosal cells, described as abetalipoproteinemia.

questions.questionText: A nascent chylomicron enters plasma. Which apoproteins does it receive from HDL?
questions.explanation: Nascent chylomicrons are secreted with ApoB-48. In plasma, they receive ApoC-II and ApoE from HDL, becoming mature functional chylomicrons.

questions.questionText: Which apoprotein activates lipoprotein lipase?
questions.explanation: ApoC-II is required for activation of LPL. Without ApoC-II, TAG-rich lipoproteins cannot be efficiently hydrolyzed in capillaries.

questions.questionText: Which apoprotein is most important for hepatic recognition of chylomicron remnants?
questions.explanation: Chylomicron remnants are taken up by the liver. Hepatic receptors recognize ApoE together with ApoB-48 on the remnant particle.

questions.questionText: A patient has ApoC-II deficiency. Which biochemical problem is expected?
options: Impaired LPL activation and accumulation of TAG-rich lipoproteins
Impaired chylomicron assembly inside intestinal cells
Increased remnant uptake by the liver
questions.explanation: ApoC-II activates LPL. If ApoC-II is deficient, LPL cannot efficiently hydrolyze TAG in chylomicrons and VLDL, causing accumulation of TAG-rich particles.

questions.questionText: A patient has lipoprotein lipase deficiency. Which plasma abnormality is most expected?
options: Absence of dietary cholesterol absorption only
questions.explanation: LPL hydrolyzes TAG in chylomicrons and VLDL. Its deficiency prevents normal clearance of TAG-rich lipoproteins, leading to severe hypertriglyceridemia.

questions.questionText: Deficiency of LPL or ApoC-II causes:
questions.explanation: The file states that deficiency of LPL or ApoC-II causes accumulation of TAG-rich lipoprotein particles in plasma, producing type I hyperlipidemia, also called familial hyperchylomicronemia.

questions.questionText: Heparin administration can help measure LPL activity because heparin:
options: Releases LPL from capillary walls into plasma
Activates pancreatic lipase in the intestine
questions.explanation: LPL is normally attached to capillary walls. Heparin can dissociate it from the endothelial surface, so LPL can be measured after heparin administration.

questions.questionText: During chylomicron metabolism, why do chylomicrons become smaller and denser?
options: Their TAG core is hydrolyzed by LPL
They gain more TAG from adipose tissue
They absorb bile salts from plasma
questions.explanation: Chylomicrons are initially TAG-rich. LPL removes TAG by hydrolysis, releasing fatty acids to tissues. As TAG content decreases, the particle becomes smaller and relatively denser.

questions.questionText: After LPL removes most TAG from chylomicrons, which particle remains?
questions.explanation: After TAG removal, chylomicrons lose ApoC-II back to HDL and become cholesterol-rich chylomicron remnants that are cleared by the liver.

questions.questionText: Which apoprotein is returned to HDL after TAG degradation in chylomicrons?
questions.explanation: ApoC-II is donated by HDL to nascent chylomicrons to activate LPL. After TAG hydrolysis, ApoC-II returns to HDL.

questions.questionText: A chylomicron remnant enters the liver. What happens next?
options: It is endocytosed, fuses with lysosomes, and is degraded to components
It returns to the intestinal lumen unchanged
It becomes a bile salt micelle directly
It becomes glucose in the blood
questions.explanation: Chylomicron remnants are taken up by hepatocytes through receptor-mediated endocytosis. They then fuse with lysosomes and are degraded into amino acids, free cholesterol, fatty acids, and glycerol.

questions.questionText: Which route correctly describes chylomicron movement after secretion?
options: Intestinal mucosal cell → lymphatic vessels → thoracic duct → blood
Intestinal mucosal cell → portal vein directly → kidney → urine
questions.explanation: Chylomicrons are large particles, so they enter lymphatic vessels first. They pass through the thoracic duct before reaching the bloodstream.

questions.questionText: Which statement best distinguishes micelles from chylomicrons clinically?
options: Micelles are needed before absorption in the intestinal lumen, while chylomicrons are formed after absorption inside mucosal cells
Chylomicrons digest lipids in the intestinal lumen, while micelles circulate in blood
Micelles require ApoB-48, while chylomicrons require bile salts only
Both are identical and only differ in name
questions.explanation: Micelles are bile salt-containing structures in the intestinal lumen that deliver digestion products to the brush border. Chylomicrons are lipoproteins assembled inside intestinal cells for transport through lymph and blood.

questions.questionText: Which statement best distinguishes chylomicrons from VLDL?
options: Chylomicrons transport exogenous dietary TAG; VLDL transports endogenous liver-made TAG
Chylomicrons are made in liver; VLDL is made in intestine
Chylomicrons contain ApoB-100; VLDL contains ApoB-48
VLDL requires bile salts for intestinal absorption
questions.explanation: Chylomicrons are intestinal particles carrying dietary fat. VLDL is produced by the liver to transport endogenously synthesized TAG to peripheral tissues.

questions.questionText: Which particle is described as the largest and least dense plasma lipoprotein?
questions.explanation: Chylomicrons contain the highest lipid percentage and the lowest protein percentage, so they are the largest and least dense lipoproteins.

questions.questionText: Which particle is the most dense plasma lipoprotein?
questions.explanation: HDL has the highest protein content and lowest TAG content among major lipoproteins, making it the most dense particle.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why lipoproteins are required in plasma?
options: Lipids are hydrophobic, so lipoproteins keep them soluble and deliver them to tissues
Lipids are highly water-soluble and need no carrier
Lipoproteins convert all lipids into glucose
questions.explanation: Many lipids are not soluble in aqueous plasma. Lipoproteins package hydrophobic lipids in a soluble particle with a surface shell of phospholipid, cholesterol, and apoproteins.

questions.questionText: Which lipoprotein structure is correct?
options: Neutral lipid core surrounded by apoproteins, phospholipid, and non-esterified cholesterol
Glycogen core surrounded by bile salts
DNA core surrounded by fatty acids
questions.explanation: Lipoproteins contain a hydrophobic core of TAG and/or cholesterol ester. Their surface contains amphipathic components that face plasma, allowing solubility.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes apoprotein function?
options: Structural components, receptor recognition sites, and enzyme activators
questions.explanation: Apoproteins stabilize lipoproteins structurally, allow receptor recognition, and activate enzymes involved in lipoprotein metabolism, such as ApoC-II activation of LPL.

questions.questionText: A patient has defective hepatic recognition of chylomicron remnants. Which apoprotein problem would most directly explain this?
questions.explanation: ApoE is critical for remnant recognition by hepatic receptors. If ApoE function is impaired, remnant clearance by the liver is reduced.

questions.questionText: Which sequence best summarizes chylomicron metabolism?
options: Nascent CM with ApoB-48 → receives ApoC-II/ApoE from HDL → LPL removes TAG → ApoC-II returns to HDL → remnant cleared by liver via ApoE/ApoB-48
Nascent CM with ApoB-100 → directly becomes LDL → cleared by kidney
Nascent CM receives bile salts → becomes micelle → enters portal vein directly
Nascent CM releases glucose → becomes HDL in intestine
questions.explanation: This is the full chylomicron pathway: intestinal formation with ApoB-48, maturation by HDL apoproteins, TAG hydrolysis by LPL, and hepatic remnant clearance.

questions.questionText: Which integrated statement best summarizes lipid digestion, absorption, and transport?
options: Dietary lipids are emulsified by bile salts, digested by pancreatic enzymes, absorbed via micelles, re-esterified in intestinal cells, packaged with ApoB-48 into chylomicrons, and delivered to tissues through LPL before hepatic remnant clearance
Dietary lipids are digested mainly in the stomach, absorbed directly as TAG into portal blood, and transported without lipoproteins
Dietary lipids are converted completely into glucose before absorption
Chylomicrons are made by liver to transport endogenous TAG to peripheral tissues
questions.explanation: This statement links the whole topic together: luminal digestion, bile salt micelles, mucosal re-esterification, chylomicron assembly and secretion, LPL-mediated TAG delivery, and remnant uptake by the liver.
